phrases.text_local: Je voudrais une IPA locale, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh voo-DRAY oon ee-pay-AH loh-KAL, seel voo PLAY.
phrases.cultural_note: Craft beer ('la bière artisanale') has exploded in France over the last decade. Mentioning that a beer is 'locale' (local) is a great conversation opener with any French brewer or beer lover.

responses.french: Bien sûr ! Notre IPA maison est très houblonnée. Vous voulez la goûter d'abord ?
responses.english: Of course! Our house IPA is very hoppy. Would you like to try it first?
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR ! notr ee-pay-AH may-ZOHN ay tray oo-bloh-NAY. voo voo-LAY lah goo-TAY dah-BOR ?

responses.french: On en a deux en ce moment. Laquelle vous tente ?
responses.english: We have two right now. Which one appeals to you?
responses.phonetic: on ahn ah DUH ahn suh moh-MAHN. lah-KEL voo TAHNT ?

phrases.text_local: Qu'est-ce que vous avez à la pression en ce moment ?
phrases.pronunciation: kes-KUH vooz ah-VAY ah lah press-YOHN ahn suh moh-MAHN ?
phrases.cultural_note: 'À la pression' means 'on draft' (literally 'under pressure'). In France, ordering 'un demi' (a half-pint of draft lager) is the classic café move.

responses.french: On a une pale ale, deux IPA, une stout et une saison en ce moment.
responses.english: We have a pale ale, two IPAs, a stout and a saison right now.
responses.phonetic: on ah oon PAL al, duh ee-pay-AH, oon STOWT ay oon say-ZOHN ahn suh moh-MAHN.

phrases.text_local: Cette bière est vraiment délicieuse. Compliments au brasseur !
phrases.pronunciation: set BYAIR ay vray-MAHN day-lee-SYUHZ. kom-plee-MAHN oh brah-SUR !
phrases.cultural_note: Complimenting the 'brasseur' (brewer) directly is always appreciated at a craft brewery. French culture values artisanal craftsmanship highly.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce que vous proposez une dégustation ? J'aimerais goûter plusieurs bières.
phrases.pronunciation: ess-KUH voo pro-poh-ZAY oon day-goos-tah-SYON ? zhay-muh-RAY goo-TAY ploo-ZYUR BYAIR.
phrases.cultural_note: 'Une dégustation' is used for tastings of wine, cheese, or beer — it implies a thoughtful, sensory experience rather than just drinking. It's a word that elevates the occasion.

responses.french: Oui ! On propose une dégustation de cinq bières, vous choisissez lesquelles.
responses.english: Yes! We offer a flight of five beers — you choose which ones.
responses.phonetic: WEE ! on pro-POZ oon day-goos-tah-SYON duh SANK BYAIR, voo shwah-zee-SAY lay-KEL.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce que vous servez à manger ici ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess-KUH voo sair-VAY ah mahn-ZHAY ee-SEE ?
phrases.cultural_note: In France, drinking without eating ('boire sans manger') is frowned upon socially — asking about food shows cultural awareness and good manners.

responses.french: On a des planches de charcuterie et quelques snacks. Le menu est là.
responses.english: We have charcuterie boards and a few snacks. The menu is right there.
responses.phonetic: on ah day PLANSH duh shar-koo-tuh-REE ay kel-KUH SNAKS. luh muh-NOO ay LAH.

responses.french: Non, mais on peut commander des pizzas d'à côté.
responses.english: No, but we can order pizzas from next door.
responses.phonetic: NOHN, may on puh koh-mahn-DAY day PEET-zah dah koh-TAY.

phrases.intent_en: Say cheers with your glass raised
phrases.cultural_note: 'Santé !' (health!) is the everyday toast. 'À la vôtre !' (to yours!) is slightly more formal and old-fashioned — using both together is perfectly charming. Always make eye contact when clinking glasses in France.
